subject_summary: A video essay by artist Brad Trimell tracing the origins, mechanics, and persistence of "art speak" (a.k.a. International Art English) — the jargon-heavy language of gallery press releases and art world criticism. Trimell argues art speak emerged from two forces: modern art's paradoxical return to literary dependency as it dematerialized (literary horseshoe theory), and the academic elite overproduction of MFA graduates competing for scarce art world positions. The essay also dissects art speak's grammatical techniques — verbification, dependent clause stacking, liminal framing — and concludes that misplaced ambition in writing must be redirected into the art itself.

key_concepts.name: Art Speak (International Art English)
key_concepts.definition: Jargon-heavy language used in art world press releases and criticism, characterized by verbification, stacked dependent clauses, liminal framing, and academic buzzwords that prioritize complexity over meaning.
key_concepts.role: Central subject of the essay; examined as a social, linguistic, and economic phenomenon

key_concepts.name: Literary Horseshoe Theory
key_concepts.definition: The irony that modern art, in rebelling against the literary narrative of Renaissance painting, paradoxically became more dependent on written text as the work itself dematerialized.
key_concepts.role: Primary historical explanation for art speak's emergence by the late 1960s

key_concepts.name: Elite Overproduction
key_concepts.definition: Peter Turchin's term for societies producing more credentialed elites than their power structures can absorb, generating competition, status anxiety, and credential inflation.
key_concepts.role: Structural explanation for why art speak escalates in complexity as BFA/MFA graduates far outnumber available jobs

key_concepts.name: Flatness
key_concepts.definition: Clement Greenberg's aesthetic ideal: a painting that exists entirely on the canvas surface with no illusionistic depth, foreground, or background.
key_concepts.role: The theoretical benchmark that triggered an arms race of dematerialization across successive art movements

key_concepts.name: Verbification
key_concepts.definition: Art speak's core technique of assigning active verbs to artworks — interrogates, queers, decolonizes, reframes — to simulate an active, transformative relationship between the object and the viewer.
key_concepts.role: Primary grammatical mechanism that creates art speak's imaginary world of artistic agency

key_concepts.name: Dematerialization
key_concepts.definition: The progressive removal of physical, pictorial, and spatial qualities from art across successive movements, from abstract expressionism through earth art and conceptual art.
key_concepts.role: The historical process that made written justification for art increasingly necessary

key_concepts.name: Credentialism
key_concepts.definition: The escalating demand for higher credentials as existing degrees lose distinguishing value: BFA to MFA to residency to PhD, in an endless cycle of status competition.
key_concepts.role: Drives art speak's increasing complexity as a proxy signal for elite educational achievement

key_concepts.name: Goodhart's Law
key_concepts.definition: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure; in art speak, incomprehensibility becomes the goal rather than an incidental feature.
key_concepts.role: Explains the perverse incentive whereby more bewildering art speak confers higher perceived status

key_concepts.name: Audience Wish Fulfillment
key_concepts.definition: Art speak's tendency to presume and dictate the audience's emotional and intellectual response in the past tense, bypassing actual viewer engagement.
key_concepts.role: Root of art speak's pretension; also reveals the absence of real audiences who care about the work

key_concepts.name: Dependent Clause Overloading
key_concepts.definition: The grammatical piling of subordinate clauses before the main clause, making sentences nearly impossible to parse orally and obscuring the subject.
key_concepts.role: Key structural technique responsible for art speak's notorious unreadability

key_concepts.name: Liminal Space
key_concepts.definition: Art speak's near-universal framing of artworks as existing between states — always in transition, spanning from one pole to another — rather than having fixed, falsifiable meaning.
key_concepts.role: Creates conceptual vagueness that resists scrutiny and invites infinite interpretation

key_concepts.name: Academic Gerontocracy
key_concepts.definition: The domination of tenured university art positions by aging baby boomer professors, blocking career pathways for younger MFA graduates and intensifying elite overproduction.
key_concepts.role: Structural bottleneck that traps artists in a cycle of credential-seeking and art speak performance

people.name: Brad Trimell
people.role: Video essayist and artist; narrator and author of The Art Speak Report
people.note: Self-described 15-year art speak user seeking recovery; shifted to plain-language communication when his work required a general audience

people.name: Tom Wolfe
people.role: American author and journalist; cultural critic of the art world
people.note: Wrote The Painted Word, describing how modern art paradoxically became dependent on written theory despite rejecting literary painting

people.name: Clement Greenberg
people.note: Championed flatness as the highest ideal of modern painting; Jackson Pollock's primary critical advocate

people.name: Jackson Pollock
people.note: His drip-painted canvases were championed by Greenberg as exemplifying flatness

people.name: Jasper Johns
people.role: American painter; key figure in Pop Art
people.note: Painted flags and numbers, achieving a meta-level of flatness that Steinberg used to argue Pop Art surpassed abstract expressionism

people.name: Leo Steinberg
people.role: Art historian and critic; champion of Pop Art
people.note: Argued abstract expressionism was still literary and Pop Art achieved a higher flatness by rendering already-flat objects flat once more

people.name: Frank Stella
people.note: Removed frames from paintings, turning the canvas shape itself into the frame — a step in art's progressive dematerialization

people.name: Sol LeWitt
people.note: Bypassed hanging pictures by painting directly on gallery walls; name transcribed as 'Solowit' in the video

people.name: Hilton Kramer
people.role: Chief art critic of The New York Times
people.note: 1974 review quoted demanding theoretical justification as a baseline requirement for exhibitions, marking art speak's shift from elite competition to universal expectation

people.name: Peter Turchin
people.note: Developed elite overproduction theory as a root cause of political instability; Trimell applies it to art speak's proliferation among over-credentialed MFA graduates

people.name: Alix Rule
people.role: Cultural critic; co-author of International Art English
people.note: Co-wrote the 2012 Triple Canopy essay analyzing art speak's linguistic patterns; name transcribed as 'Alex Rool' in the video

people.name: David Levine
people.role: Performance artist; co-author of International Art English
people.note: Co-wrote the 2012 Triple Canopy essay with Alix Rule documenting art speak's wordiness and syntactic habits

books.title: The Painted Word
books.author: Tom Wolfe
books.note: Argues modern art became paradoxically literary and theory-dependent; source for the literary horseshoe theory; title transcribed as 'The Painter Word' in the video

books.title: International Art English
books.author: Alix Rule and David Levine
books.note: Essay published in Triple Canopy (online); linguistic analysis of art speak's characteristic wordiness, syntax, and vocabulary; no ISBN

movements.name: Renaissance Painting
movements.description: Literary, illusionistic painting illustrating biblical stories and historical events with realistic depth, positioning the viewer as if entering the scene — the tradition modern art defined itself against

movements.name: Modernism
movements.description: Broad movement rejecting illusionism and literary narrative; artists made the canvas surface and means of construction visible, setting off the dematerialization arms race

movements.name: Abstract Expressionism
movements.description: American movement defined by gestural brushwork and the drip technique (Pollock); championed by Greenberg for embodying flatness, though later critics called it still too literary

movements.name: Pop Art
movements.description: Used already-flat commercial imagery (flags, numbers) to achieve a meta-level of flatness that, per Leo Steinberg, defeated abstract expressionism at its own game

movements.name: Color Field Painting
movements.description: Removed gestural brushstrokes from abstract painting, eliminating the personal narrative implied by the painter's hand

movements.name: Minimalism
movements.description: Stripped art to geometric forms that divided and defined gallery space itself, turning the entire building into an extension of the artwork

movements.name: Land Art (Earth Art)
movements.description: Removed the gallery building by creating permanent large-scale works outdoors, requiring written documentation for the work to be known at all

movements.name: Conceptual Art
movements.description: Removed permanent physical objects; work could exist as documentation of an idea or instructions — the movement most reliant on written text and most responsible for institutionalizing art speak

movements.name: MFA Academic Art World
movements.description: The expansion of BFA and MFA programs replaced Bohemian culture with academic critique culture, making art speak the lingua franca and universities the art world's primary economic engine

movements.name: Postmodern Theory in Art
movements.description: French postmodern philosophy and New Left identity politics filtered into MFA curricula, providing the vocabulary — subaltern, the political, radicality, liminality — that forms art speak's DNA

quotes: by instantiating a self-reflective discourse of communicative modalities, we can equitably interrogate liminal notions of difference within writerly technologies

Realism does not lack its partisans, but it does lack a persuasive theory, and given the nature of our intellectual commerce with works of art, to lack a persuasive theory is to lack something crucial. — Hilton Kramer, New York Times, 1974
Her canvases adopt an interplay of repetition and variety, exploring the relationship between the digital and the gestural within the medium of painting.
The photographs resist the instantaneous and resided a place of tension, striving to poetically reframe an essential part of the mystery of a global tribe.
encoding blackness as a para-history, the sculpture radically decolonizes the white cube as a racially homogeneous space.
This harbor of aesthetic ambiguity stretches across temporalities, transforming viewers gaze with nuanced visual enunciations.

Working with such quasi-absolute practices, which speak of past economies and ecologies, could easily culminate in conservative and retroactive proposals, but the work successfully avoids a nostalgic rescue and or fetishization of these modes of production.

Whether clinging to the wall or freely suspended, these objects turn codified binaries beyond expectation. They are visceral, yet epidermal, prosthetic, yet native, sensuous, yet aversive, permeable, yet plasticine.
Like an insect, or the wounded, or even a fugitive, the artist moves forward with their signature combination of skill and awkwardness.

veering away from linear and deterministic communication processes while passing along the curvatures of a matrix, these sequences of poetic computation are a choreography of visceral compositions arriving from algorithmic rituals.
The medium is the message and the message is pick me.
What arts speak offers is a chance to imitate the symptom of genius without replicating the cause to create the same confusion but without the artistic payoff.
